title: Mathematicians Shatter 150-Year-Old Geometry Rule with Torus Discovery
summary: A longstanding principle in geometry, established by Pierre Ossian Bonnet over 150 years ago, has been challenged by recent research. Bonnet's rule asserted that for compact surfaces, knowing the metric (which describes distances along the surface) and mean curvature (which indicates how the surface bends in space) at every point uniquely determines the surface's shape. Mathematicians from the Technical University of Munich, Technical University of Berlin, and North Carolina State University constructed two compact, self-contained torus-shaped surfaces that share identical metric and mean curvature values, yet their global structures differ. This provides the first explicit counterexample to Bonnet's rule for such surfaces, resolving a problem that had persisted for decades. Previously, exceptions were known only for non-compact surfaces, but this work demonstrates that even closed, doughnut-like tori can defy the rule, highlighting that local measurement data may not always specify a unique global shape.

background_read: Geometry is a branch of mathematics focused on shapes, sizes, and properties of space. Differential geometry, in particular, uses calculus to study curves and surfaces. Pierre Ossian Bonnet, a 19th-century French mathematician, proposed a rule that for compact surfaces—closed, bounded shapes like spheres or tori—knowing the metric (distances) and mean curvature (bending) at every point should uniquely determine the surface's shape. This principle guided geometric thinking for over 150 years, with known exceptions only for non-compact surfaces, such as infinite planes. The recent discovery by an international team of mathematicians constructs two torus-shaped surfaces with identical local properties but different global forms, challenging this rule and advancing our understanding of surface geometry.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The article reports that mathematicians have found a counterexample to Bonnet's 150-year-old rule by constructing two torus-shaped surfaces with identical metric and mean curvature but different global shapes. Purpose: To resolve a long-standing problem in differential geometry and demonstrate that local measurement data may not uniquely determine a surface's overall structure. Evidence Evaluation: The evidence is strong, based on explicit mathematical construction by researchers from reputable universities, though it is a theoretical result without experimental data. Author Credibility: The authors are professors and mathematicians from Technical University of Munich, Technical University of Berlin, and North Carolina State University, indicating expertise in geometry and topology. Methodology: The researchers used mathematical modeling and construction to create the torus examples, applying principles of differential geometry to challenge existing theories.

perspectives.description: This discovery represents a significant breakthrough in mathematics, refining our understanding of geometric principles and inspiring further research.
